questions: Should the audio analysis be computed eagerly (all frames up-front when audio clips load) or lazily (on-demand per frame)? Eager is simpler and deterministic for rendering but has a startup cost proportional to timeline length. Lazy is faster to start but may cause frame drops during playback. The plan assumes eager/offline analysis via OfflineAudioContext.

For the EffectCreatorPanel preview: is a synthetic oscillating signal sufficient, or should users be able to pick an audio file to preview with? The plan proposes synthetic-only for the initial implementation to keep scope small.

Should the audio-binding parameter type produce a resolved numeric value at render time (i.e., the effect receives a number that's already mapped from the audio source), or should effects receive the raw AudioBindingValue config and do the mapping themselves? The plan assumes the latter (effects receive the config object) since the useAudioReactive hook already provides the raw audio data.

What FFT size / number of frequency bins should be used? The plan assumes 256 bins as a reasonable default for visual effects. Higher bin counts give more frequency resolution but more data per frame.

success_criteria.criterion: All existing DynamicEffectRegistry.test.tsx tests pass without modification
Compiled effects that do NOT call useAudioReactive() work identically to before (no behavioral change)
useAudioReactive() returns zeroed/silent AudioAnalysisData when no AudioAnalysisProvider exists in the tree
useAudioReactive() returns frame-synchronized audio data (indexed by useCurrentFrame()) when AudioAnalysisProvider is present
wrapWithEffect and wrapWithClipEffects in effects/index.tsx are not modified
EffectComponentProps type does not gain audio-related props
ParameterType union includes 'audio-binding' and ParameterControls renders corresponding UI
validateAndCoerceParams correctly validates and coerces audio-binding parameter values
effectPromptTemplate.ts documents useAudioReactive() with return type and usage examples
AudioAnalysisProvider wraps the composition in TimelineRenderer.tsx so all effects can access audio data
EffectCreatorPanel preview works for audio-reactive effects (synthetic signal when no audio present)
All 11 entrance, 6 exit, and 5 continuous built-in effects are unaffected
No new Remotion packages added — uses Web Audio API only
AudioAnalysisProvider new file is under ~300 lines
Beat detection uses energy-threshold with cooldown to avoid false positives
Audio analysis is pre-computed (offline) for deterministic rendering
Audio-binding parameter UI shows source selector (bass/mid/treble/amplitude) and min/max range controls
Manual smoke test: create a custom effect using useAudioReactive() in the effect creator, preview shows movement with synthetic audio
Manual smoke test: add audio track to timeline, apply audio-reactive continuous effect, playback shows effect responding to actual audio

assumptions: Eager/offline audio analysis via OfflineAudioContext is the right approach — pre-compute all frames on audio load rather than real-time analysis. This gives deterministic renders and avoids frame drops.
Web Audio API is available in all target environments (modern browsers). No need for a polyfill.
256 FFT bins is a reasonable default for visual effects. This can be made configurable later if needed.
Synthetic oscillating signal is sufficient for EffectCreatorPanel preview — no audio file picker needed for initial implementation.

The audio-binding parameter stores its config as an object ({ source, min, max }) and effects use the raw useAudioReactive() data to map it. The parameter system doesn't auto-resolve audio bindings to numeric values.
Audio clip src URLs (from assetEntry.src) are same-origin or CORS-enabled, since they're already being used for playback via Remotion's Audio component.
The OfflineAudioContext can handle mixing multiple audio tracks with different start times, volumes, trim points, and playback rates.
The AudioAnalysisProvider can be a no-op wrapper (providing silent data) when there are no audio clips, with negligible performance overhead.
